metadata.title: Writer/Reviewer: Same Model, Clean Context
metadata.description_blurb: The session that wrote the grading tool contains all the writer's reasoning — a reviewer subagent with a clean context has never seen those decisions and applies fresh judgment, catching what same-context review misses.

beats.narration_text: She asked Claude to review the grading tool it had just written. Same session, same context. Claude said the code looked correct. It was not — there was an off-by-one error in the rubric parser that would misgrade any submission over twenty items. The session that wrote the tool contained all of the writer's reasoning. The reviewer validated the logic it had already accepted. This is Salam, Liam — in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, review the grading tool same session

props.command: review the grading tool for correctness

beats.narration_text: Same-context review is the most common subagent failure to notice. The reviewer is the writer. It re-reads the code in the context of every decision that produced it — the assumptions, the constraints that felt obvious, the paths not taken that were implicitly ruled out. The review validates the logic the writer already accepted. What the writer's context normalizes, the writer's review cannot flag.
beats.new_visual_element: Same-context problem — review inherits all writer assumptions / cannot flag what writer normalized

beats.narration_text: A subagent is a Claude session spawned with its own context window, its own system prompt, and its own allowed tools. It reads what it needs, returns a summary to the main session, and stops. The main session never sees the subagent's intermediate work — it sees only the summary. For the Writer/Reviewer pattern, the reviewer subagent reads the finished code with no context of how it was written. It has never seen the decisions.
beats.new_visual_element: Subagent mechanics — own context / own tools / returns summary / main session stays clean

beats.narration_text: The reviewer subagent catches what same-context review misses: assumptions the writer made that are wrong for some inputs, edge cases the writer tested but the test did not reach, and the off-by-one error that is only visible when you have not been staring at the loop for two hours. It also catches the implicit design decisions — the rubric parser assumes items are zero-indexed. That assumption is invisible in the writer session. The reviewer, reading the function cold, asks whether that is stated anywhere.
beats.new_visual_element: What reviewer catches — implicit assumptions / untested edge cases / undocumented constraints

beats.narration_text: The Writer/Reviewer pattern is not about distrust — it is about context pollution. The writer session is the wrong instrument for finding what the writer normalized. The reviewer subagent is the correct instrument because its context is empty. Same model. Same weights. Different starting context. The starting context is what determines which errors are visible and which are beneath the surface.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — same model / different context / reviewer finds what writer cannot

beats.narration_text: Run a reviewer on your next build with this prompt in a clean session.

props.command: [Open a new Claude Code session] You are a code reviewer. You have no context of how this code was written. Read these files: [list files]. Identify: (1) assumptions in the code that are not documented anywhere, (2) inputs or edge cases the code does not handle, (3) any implicit constraint that would only be visible to someone reading this cold. Do not ask about design intent — report what you see in the code.

beats.narration_text: If this catches the error the writer's context made invisible, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
